coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a promising material currently being explored in many industrial applications in the biomedical and agri-food areas; however, studies supporting the environmental health risk assessment of CDs are needed. This study focuses on various CD forms including iron (FeCD) and copper (CuCD) doped CDs synthesized using hydrothermal method, their fate in gastrointestinal tract, and their cytotoxicity and potential changes to cellular metabolome in a triculture small intestinal epithelial model. Physicochemical characterization revealed that 75% of Fe in FeCD and 95% of Cu in CuCD were dissolved during digestion. No significant toxic effects were observed for pristine CDs and FeCDs. However, CuCD induced significant dose-dependent toxic effects including decreases in TEER and cell viability, increases in cytotoxicity and ROS production, and alterations in important metabolites, including d-glucose, L-cysteine, uridine, citric acid and multiple fatty acids. These results support the current understanding that pristine CDs are relatively non-toxic and the cytotoxicity is dependent on the doping molecules.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the study design.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of carbon dots (CD), iron doped carbon dots (FeCD) and copper doped carbon dots (CuCD).
Electron microscopy of CDs: (A) CD, (B) FeCD, (C) CuCD.
(A) Volume-weighted size distributions for particles in the small intestinal digesta of FFM, CD, FeCD, and CuCD. (B) Dissolution% of Fe and Cu in the small intestinal digesta of FeCD and CuCD.

In vitro toxicity of ingested CDs. (A) TEER measurements after 24 h exposures to digesta from simulated digestion. (B) Cell viability following 24 h exposure to digesta from simulated digestion. (C) Cytotoxicity (LDH assay) following 24 h exposure to digesta from simulated digestion. (D) ROS generation (fold change from untreated cells) following 6 h exposure to digesta from simulated digestion. The starting concentrations of CDs in FFM at 0.1% and 1% w/w correspond to administered concentrations of approximately 20 and 200 μg/mL after successive physiological dilutions in simulated digestion and cell culture medium. Error bars represent ± standard deviation. The statistical significance (P-value) of the interaction effect between FFM treatment and CDs treatment on cytotoxicity was determined by two-way ANOVA analysis. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.
Effects of ingested CDs on cellular metabolites in triculture small intestinal epithelial cells.
Effects of ingested CDs on cellular proteomics in triculture small intestinal epithelial cells.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a promising material currently being explored in many industrial applications in the biomedical and agri-food areas; however, studies supporting the environmental health risk assessment of CDs are needed. This study focuses on various CD forms including iron (FeCD) and copper (CuCD) doped CDs synthesized using hydrothermal method, their fate in gastrointestinal tract, and their cytotoxicity and potential changes to cellular metabolome in a triculture small intestinal epithelial model. Physicochemical characterization revealed that 75% of Fe in FeCD and 95% of Cu in CuCD were dissolved during digestion. No significant toxic effects were observed for pristine CDs and FeCDs. However, CuCD induced significant dose-dependent toxic effects including decreases in TEER and cell viability, increases in cytotoxicity and ROS production, and alterations in important metabolites, including d-glucose, L-cysteine, uridine, citric acid and multiple fatty acids. These results support the current understanding that pristine CDs are relatively non-toxic and the cytotoxicity is dependent on the doping molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: Engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) have shown great potential in the agri-food industry, addressing issues in food safety, quality, agrichemical delivery and sustainability (DeLoid et al., 2018; Vaze et al., 2019; Eleftheriadou et al., 2017; Schoepf et al., 2017; Pyrgiotakis et al., 2018; Sampathkumar et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020a; Rodrigues et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2021). Examples include titanium dioxide, silica, silver, biopolymers, and carbon-based nanomaterials which are currently being investigated for agri-food applications such as antimicrobial packaging materials, food additives, nanofertilizers and biosensors (Sampathkumar et al., 2019; Bindraban et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020; Aytac et al., 2020).

Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of carbon-based ENMs with ultrafine sizes below 10 nm, have gained increasing research attention due to their impressive photostability, tunable surface functionalization, good water solubility, and biocompatibility in certain biological systems (Wang and Qiu, 2016; Zuo et al., 2016; Qu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2019). CDs have widespread potential applications, including bio-imaging, biomedicine, biosensors, photocatalysis and drug delivery (Wang and Qiu, 2016; Zuo et al., 2016). In particular, CDs have been shown to improve food quality and safety (Qu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2019), including the detection of nutrients (Liu et al., 2016; Chandra et al., 2019), pesticides (Wang et al., 2018; Mandal et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018a), pathogenic microorganisms (Nandi et al., 2015; Ahmadian-Fard-Fini et al., 2018; Bhattacharya et al., 2017), banned additives (Xu et al., 2015), etc.

Recently, CDs have shown potential for application in sustainable agriculture to enhance plant growth rate and overall yield, as well as for disease resistance. For example, CDs can enhance photosynthesis, promoting lettuce growth after foliar application with CDs as a light fertilizer (Xu et al., 2020b). CDs were also shown to enhance the production yield of crops by increasing nutrient root absorption, (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2015; Zheng et al., 2017) through accelerating photosynthesis and nutrient assimilation, and by enhancing resistance of crops to abiotic/biotic stress (Li et al., 2020; Li et al., 2018b). It is worth noting that the easy surface functionalization of CDs affords great potential as a promising carrier of micronutrients into plants, such as beneficial metal nutrient elements, Fe and Cu, which are essential for normal plant growth and development.

The applications of CDs in biomedicine, food industry, and agriculture will inevitably result in human oral exposure, however, risk assessment of CDs under these scenarios are needed. The potential biological implications of CDs and other ENMs on human cannot be predicted based merely on their physical morphological and chemical characteristics (Sohal et al., 2018a; Sohal et al., 2018b). Their size, shape, agglomeration state, and the formation of biomolecular coronas all affect the bio-interactions of ENMs with human cells and tissues, and thereby alter their potential toxicity (McClements et al., 2016; McClements and Xiao, 2017; Cao et al., 2019a; DeLoid et al., 2019; Cao et al., 2020a; Cao et al., 2016; Cao et al., 2019b; Bitounis et al., 2020; Sohal et al., 2020; Coreas et al., 2020).

Studies of CDs exposure in various cell lines and animal models are limited and have found no evidence of significant toxicity (Liu et al., 2020; Raja et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2011; Havrdova et al., 2016). More importantly, there are no published studies on the fate of CDs in the gastrointestinal tract (GIT) and potential associated toxicity.

In this study, the transformations of CD, iron doped (FeCD) and copper doped (CuCD) CDs in the GIT were assessed using state of the art analytical methods. Furthermore, the impact of ingested CDs on small intestinal cellular health, as well as the metabolomic and proteomic profile, was assessed using an integrated methodology recently developed by the authors which takes into account the effects of food matrix and digestion (DeLoid et al., 2017) (Fig. 1 ). Food models, with or without CDs, were digested using a three-phase (oral, gastric, small intestinal) GIT simulator, and the final digesta was applied to a small intestinal epithelial triculture cellular model for toxicity assessment. Untargeted metabolomics analysis was performed using gas chromatography mass-spectrometry (GC–MS), and liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) was employed to determine the cellular proteomic response to ingested CDs. This study adds important information to our understanding of the potential cellular impacts of ingested CDs under physiologically relevant conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CD), iron doped CDs (FeCD) and copper doped CDs (CuCD)
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 , to prepare CD, FeCD and CuCD, 0.6 g of citric acid with or without 15% wt/wt FeCl3, or 16% wt/wt CuCl2 were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. These solutions were transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After heating at 200 °C for 10 h, the CDs solutions were allowed to cool down to room temperature and the solutions were subsequently centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 mins. The precipitate was discarded, and the pH of the supernatants were measured and adjusted to 6.5–7.0. The pH-adjusted solutions were kept for further characterization.

The UV–visible spectrum was measured with a Perkin Elmer Lambda 750 UV–vis spectrophotometer, while the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum were obtained with Varian Spectrum GX spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) was measured with a Horiba Jobin Yvon (FluoroMax 4) Luminescence Spectrometer. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was measured by a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg Kα X-ray (hν = 1283.3 eV). The particle size and zeta potential were measured by ZEN3690 zetasizer (Malvern, U.K.). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images and EDX mapping were collected collected by a TEM Jeol 2010 UHR (200 kV) and average diameter (d TEM) of the particles were analyzed using ImageJ.

The in vitro oral, gastric, small intestinal phase digestion of the CD samples is outlined in Fig. 1. The digestion protocol has been described in our previous publications (DeLoid et al., 2017). In summary, samples consisting of (1) fasting food model (FFM, i.e., water) alone (control), (2) FFM + 0.1% wt/wt CD, (3) FFM + 1% wt/wt CD, (4) FFM + 0.1% wt/wt FeCD, (5) FFM + 1% wt/wt FeCD, (6) FFM + 0.1% wt/wt CuCD, and (7) FFM + 1% wt/wt CuCD were subjected to in vitro three-phase (oral, gastric, and small intestinal) digestion. Samples were brought to 37 °C, combined 1:1 with simulated saliva fluid, and inverted for 10 s to mimic agitation in the mouth. The resulting oral phase digesta were then combined 1:1 with simulated gastric fluid and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h in a shaking incubator at 200 RPM to complete the gastric phase. The gastric digesta were then combined with salts, bile extract and lipase to simulate intestinal fluid, and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, while maintaining a constant pH of 7.0, using a pH stat titration device (TitroLine® 7000, SI Analytics, GmbH, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-angle laser diffraction particle size measurements of simulated digesta
ce:section.ce:para: A laser diffraction particle size analyzer (Mastersizer 3000, Malvern Instruments, Ltd) equipped with a wet dispersion unit (Hydro SV) and 633 and 466 nm laser source was used to measure the volume-weighted particle size distributions in food models and in oral, gastric and small intestinal phase digesta. The samples were diluted with double-distilled water and measured while stirred at 1800 rpm. Five measurements of 120 s (at 633 nm) and 10 s (at 466 nm) each were performed. The volume-weighted size distributions were averaged using the built-in software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ICP-OES for Fe and Cu dissolution in small intestinal digesta
ce:section.ce:para: Dissolved Fe and Cu were determined in the simulated digesta of FeCD and CuCD at 1% wt/wt in FFM by ultracentrifugation at 50,000 ×g for one hour. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis were performed on the supernatant samples to confirm that the CDs were removed by ultracentrifuging. The concentrations of Cu and Fe in the whole simulated digesta and the supernatant were determined using inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Thermo Fisher Scientific iCAP 6500). Samples were hot block digested (115 °C, 45 min) with 500 μL trace-metal free HNO3, then with 500 μL 30% H2O2(115 °C, 25 min). Following digestion, samples were diluted with Millipore water (18 mΩ) and thoroughly mixed. The dissolution% was calculated with the amount of dissolved Cu/Fe measured in supernatant and the amount of total Cu/Fe measured in the whole digesta.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and exposure of triculture small intestinal epithelium
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation and characterization of the in vitro triculture small intestinal epithelial model used in this study has been described in our previous publication (DeLoid et al., 2017). Briefly, 1.5 mL of a 3:1 mixture of Caco-2 and HT29-MTX cells (Sigma-Aldrich, Inc.) at a total concentration of 3 × 105 cells/mL in DMEM with 10% FBS was seeded into the apical compartments of collagen-coated 0.4 μm pore size, 24 mm diameter polycarbonate transwell inserts, and 2.5 mL of complete DMEM media was added to the basolateral compartments. Cells were incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Media was changed at 4 days and every other day thereafter, until day 15. On days 15 and 16, the basolateral media was replaced with 2.5 mL of a suspension of Raji B cells (feeder cells which induce some Caco-2 cells to assume an M-cell phenotype) at a concentration of 1 × 106 cells/mL in 1:1 DMEM: RPMI complete media. Fluorescence immunostaining of mucin (MUC2) and the M-cell marker glycoprotein-2 (GP2) and confocal imaging revealed a continuous epithelial layer with scattered mucin-rich cells (HT29-MTX cells) in nests of enterocytes (Fig. S8), resembling an in vivo small intestinal epithelium (DeLoid et al., 2017). Cells were exposed on day 17 by replacing the apical media with 1.5 mL of the final small intestinal phase digesta (prepared as described above) diluted at a 1:3 ratio with DMEM media. Cells were exposed for 24 h at 37 °C.

Transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) was measured using an EVOM2 Epithelial Volt/Ohm Meter with a Chopstick Electrode Set (World Precision Instruments). Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release was measured in transwell cell supernatants at 24 h using the Pierce LDH assay kit (Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA). ROS production was assessed using the CellROX® green reagent (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc.) after 6 h exposure and cells treated with 100 μM menadione for 1 h at 37 °C were used as positive controls. Cell viability was assessed using the PrestoBlue™ reagent (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc., Waltham, MA), after 24 h exposure, in 96-well co-culture plates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for metabolomics and proteomics
ce:section.ce:para: Metabolites and proteins were simultaneously extracted from small intestinal epithelial cell pellets using the MPLEx protocol, a modified Folch extraction method, as described previously (Nakayasu et al., 2016). Briefly, 150 μL of lysis buffer (100 mM NH4HCO3, pH 8.0) was added to the cell pellets in 1.5-mL Eppendorf Safe-Lock tube. The volume of the lysis buffer was approximately thrice the volume of the cell pellet. Fifty μL of 0.1 mm Zirconia/Silica beads and 750 μL of 2:1 chloroform: methanol was added to each tube, which is equivalent to a solvent ratio of 3:8:4 H2O:CHCl3:CH3OH. The samples were vortexed for 30 s, chilled on an ice block for 5 min, and vortexed again for 30 s. The samples were centrifuged at 14,000 ×g for 10 min at 4 °C to separate the upper aqueous phase, protein interphase, and lower organic phase. The upper aqueous layer and the lower organic layer were combined in glass vials (ALS vials, MicroSolv), and completely dried in a speed-vac concentrator (Labconco CentriVap Benchtop Vacuum Concentrator) for subsequent metabolomics (GC–MS) analysis. The protein pellet was stored at −80 °C until further processing.

Dried extracts were chemically derivatized using a modified version of the protocol used to create FiehnLib (Kind et al., 2009). Briefly, dried metabolite extracts were dried again to remove any residual water if they had been stored at −80°C. To protect carbonyl groups and reduce the number of tautomeric isomers, 20 μl of methoxyamine in pyridine (30 mg ml−1) was added to each sample, followed by vortexing for 30 s and incubation at 37 °C with vigorous shaking (1000 rpm) for 90 min. The sample vials were then inverted once to capture any condensation of solvent at the cap surface, followed by a brief centrifugation at 1000 ×g for 1 min. To derivatize hydroxyl and amine groups to trimethylsilylated (TMS) forms, 80 μl of N-methyl-N-(trimethylsilyl)trifluoroacetamide (MSTFA) with 1% trimethylchlorosilane (TMCS) were then added to each vial, followed by vortexing for 10 s and incubation at 37 °C with shaking (1000 rpm) for 30 min. Again, the sample vials were inverted once, followed by centrifugation at 1000 ×g for 5 min. The samples were allowed to cool to room temperature and analyzed the same day.

An Agilent GC 7890A coupled with a single quadrupole MSD 5975C (Agilent Technologies) was used and the samples were blocked and analyzed in random order for each experiment. An HP-5MS column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm; Agilent Technologies) was used for untargeted metabolomics analyses. The sample injection mode was splitless and 1 μl of each sample was injected. The injection port temperature was held at 250 °C throughout the analysis. The GC oven was held at 60 °C for 1 min after injection and the temperature was then increased to 325 °C by 10 °C min−1, followed by a 5 min hold at 325 °C (Kim et al., 2013). The helium gas flow rates for each experiment were determined by the Agilent Retention Time Locking function based on analysis of deuterated myristic acid and were in the range of 0.45–0.5 ml min−1. Data were collected over the mass range 50–550 m/z. A mixture of fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs) (C8–C28) was analyzed once per day together with the samples for retention index alignment purposes during subsequent data analysis.

GC–MS raw data file processing was done using Metabolite Detector software and metabolites were identified by matching experimental spectra and retention indices to an augmented version of FiehnLib (Kind et al., 2009). All identifications were manually validated to reduce deconvolution errors and to eliminate false identifications. The NIST 14 GC–MS library was also used to cross-validate the spectral matching scores obtained using the Agilent library and to provide identifications of unmatched metabolites.

Protein pellets from the mPLEX extraction were dissolved in 100 μL of dissolving buffer (8 M urea in 100 mM NH4HCO3) with sonication and vortexing. The BCA assay was used to determine the protein concentration. For each sample, an aliquot of 100 μg proteins was diluted with 25 mM NH4HCO3 seven-fold to urea concentration of 1 M. Two μg of trypsin was added, and digestion was performed at 37 °C for overnight. The resulting peptides were purified by solid phase extraction with C18 columns and dried by speedvac. Dried peptide samples were stored in −80 °C until LC-MS/MS analysis.

For proteomic analysis, dried peptides were first dissolved in 0.1% formic acid and then quantified by BCA. The peptide solution was adjusted to 0.1 μg/μL and 5 μL was loaded for LC-MS/MS analysis. The LC gradients and data acquisition parameters for proteomics can be found elsewhere (Zhang et al., 2020a; Cao et al., 2020b). Raw data processing by MaxQuant and the following statistical analysis in R were performed as described previously (Cao et al., 2020a).

All experiments, with the exception of metabolomics and proteomics, were performed in triplicate. Four biological replicates were used in the metabolomic and proteomic analysis. Results of TEER, cell viability, cytotoxicity and ROS generation studies were analyzed by two-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparisons test.

A robust Mahalanobis distance based on metabolite abundance vectors (rMd-PAV) was calculated to identify potential sample outliers in the data (Matzke MM 2011 https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btr479). No outliers were found and therefore no samples were removed from the dataset. The data was first log2 transformed. A global median normalization was applied to each sample in the data. For each metabolite, an ANOVA model was fit and two pairwise comparisons were conducted, depending on data availability: CD vs FFM and CuCD vs FFM. Within a metabolite, p-values for pairwise comparisons were adjusted using a single-step adjustment method to account for multiple comparisons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of pristine CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized using a conventional hydrothermal method (Tajik et al., 2020; Wang and Hu, 2014; Das et al., 2018; Thangaraj et al., 2019) with citric acid as shown in Fig. 2 and the yield was 81%. Iron (Fe) and copper (Cu) were selected as dopants given the potential future application of CDs in sustainable agriculture as a promising carrier of nutrients, which are essential for normal plant growth and development. The Fe and Cu content in doped CDs was determined by ICP-OES and was ~13.5% and 21.3% wt/wt, respectively (Table S1). The presence of Fe and Cu in the CDs was further verified by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) which is shown in Fig. S1. Based on the XPS fitting in Fig. S2, the peaks at 925.3 eV and 711.6 eV could be assigned to Fe3+2p1/2 and Fe3+2p3/2, while the peaks at 923.7 eV and 710.1 eV is attributed to Fe2+2p1/2 and Fe2+2p3/2. The coexistence of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in FeCD indicated there is partial reduction of Fe3+ while the reason is not clear and calls for further study. In the fitted core level spectra of Cu 2p (Fig. S3), the peaks at 953.3 eV and 933.3 eV could be assigned to Cu2+2p1/2 and Cu2+2p3/2, which indicated the only oxidation state in CuCD is Cu2+.

The highlighted stretch vibration peaks of CC from alkene/conjugated alkene groups and aromatic CH in the FTIR spectra (Fig. S4) confirmed the successful generation of CD, FeCD and CuCD. The EDX mapping of FeCD and CuCD (Figs. S5 and S6) indicated the presence of all incorporated elements such as Fe/Cu, O and Cl. The unanimous distribution of FeCD/CuCD morphology and all elements mapping implied that the nutrients existed in carbon dot as dopants rather than impurities. The TEM images of prepared CD, FeCD and CuCD are presented in Fig. 3 . The cluster observed in Fig. 3B suggests that the produced FeCD are less stable and tends to aggregate. The particle diameters measured by TEM (d TEM) were 4.9 ± 1.25 nm for CD and 6.8 ± 1.90 nm for CuCD (Table S1). The size of CD, CuCD and FeCD cluster were verified by Dynamic light scattering (DLS); the hydrodynamic diameters (d H) were 21 ± 1 nm, 8 ± 1 nm and 127 ± 2 nm respectively (Table S1). The Zeta potentials (ζ-potentials) for CD, FeCD and CuCD were − 45.9 mV, −34.2 mV and − 47.5 mV, respectively (Table S1).

Fig. S7 shows the UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of CD, FeCD and CuCD at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. Due to the generation of aromatic structures, CD is emissive. Similarly, photoluminescence can be observed from FeCD and CuCD. The presence of metal ion substantially quenches the photoluminescence; fluorescence intensity is thus much lower for FeCD and CuCD compared to CD. The photoluminescence quenching by metal ions is in agreement with other published studies (Wang et al., 2015; Anjali Devi et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Agglomeration of CDs and dissolution of Fe and Cu in GIT
ce:section.ce:para: The behavior of CDs in the GIT is important for understanding their biological fate and potential toxicity. The biotransformation of nanomaterials in the GIT is a strong determinant of bioactivity and toxicity (McClements et al., 2016; McClements and Xiao, 2017; Cao et al., 2019a; DeLoid et al., 2019; Cao et al., 2020a; Cao et al., 2016; Cao et al., 2019b; Bitounis et al., 2020; Sohal et al., 2020; Coreas et al., 2020). This study evaluated the agglomeration of CDs and the dissolution of Fe and Cu in GIT. The volume-weighted particle size distributions of small intestinal phase digesta from FFM (i.e., water), CD, FeCD, and CuCD, are shown in Fig. 4A. It should be noted that the results represent a heterogeneous mixture of particles, including a variety of agglomerated digestive proteins, and that the technique used for this size distribution analysis (Multi-angle laser diffraction or MALD) does not distinguish among these different types of particles or indicate which particles either correspond to or contain CDs. The size distribution of digesta from FFM alone featured a single dominant, broad peak (~ 4 μm) with prominent shoulder or adjoining minor peak (~11 μm). These particles are most likely agglomerated digestive proteins from GIT digestion. On the other hand, the size distributions of small intestinal phase digesta containing the CD, FeCD, or CuCD shows a dominant major peak at about 3 μm which could be mainly agglomerated digestive proteins, and a smaller peak at about 0.5 μm which is likely the agglomerated CDs.

Dissolution of Fe and Cu in the small intestinal digesta of FeCD and CuCD are shown in Fig. 4B; 49% and 75% of Fe were dissolved during GIT digestion of FeCD at starting concentrations of 0.1% and 1% wt/wt in FFM, respectively. As expected, the dissolution of Fe from FeCD is dose-dependent and the higher starting food concentration of FeCD has greater Fe dissolution. Conversely, 92% and 95% of Cu were dissolved during GIT digestion of CuCD at starting concentrations of 0.1% and 1% wt/wt in FFM, respectively. Data suggests that most of Cu in CuCD was dissolved during GIT digestion and is clearly more readily dissolved than Fe in FeCD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicological assessment of CDs in a triculture intestinal epithelial cell model
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro cellular toxicity was evaluated for small intestinal digesta from the FFM control (water), CD, FeCD, and CuCD at starting food concentrations of 0.1% and 1% wt/wt. It is important to note that due to the successive physiological dilutions of the initial food model suspensions in the oral, gastric, and small intestinal phases of simulated digestion (a total dilution to ~1/12), and an additional 1/4 dilution of the final digesta in cell culture media, the starting concentrations of CD in FFM at 0.1% and 1% wt/wt correspond to administered cellular concentrations of 20.8 and 208.3 μg/mL, respectively.

The results of toxicological assessment are summarized in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5A and B, compared with digesta from the FFM control, the digesta containing CD and FeCD at either concentration of 0.1% and 1% wt/wt and CuCD at concentration of 0.1% wt/wt, caused no significant changes in either transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) or cell viability, indicating that cell layer integrity and tight junctions were not impaired, and that cells remained metabolically active. In contrast, CuCD at 1% wt/wt induced a slight but statistically significant decrease in the TEER (p < 0.01), indicating the cell layer integrity was damaged. Moreover, CuCD at 1% wt/wt dramatically decreased the cell viability (p < 0.001), resulting in only 60% of cells remaining metabolically active. As shown in Fig. 4B, over 90% of Cu in CuCD was dissolved during GIT digestion. It has been reported that Cu ions cause dose-dependent decrease in TEER values and cell viability in Caco-2 cell monolayers (Ude et al., 2017). Therefore, cytotoxicity was presumably induced by the dissolved Cu in CuCD.

Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) release from tricultures was measured after 24 h of exposure to assess acute cytotoxicity. As shown in Fig. 5C, digesta of FFM alone, CD and FeCD at either concentration caused no increases in cytotoxicity. For CuCD, dose dependent and statistically significant cytotoxicity was observed; 9.5% and 44.4% increases in toxicity for the starting food concentrations of 0.1% and 1% wt/wt, respectively. Importantly, the LDH results are in accordance with the above cell viability results.

ROS production relative to untreated controls (incubated with fresh culture medium) after 6 h exposure are shown in Fig. 5D. The positive control (menadione, 100 μM) induced a 2.4-fold increase (p < 0.001) in ROS production. Digesta of FFM alone (control), CD and FeCD at either concentration of 0.1% or 1% wt/wt caused no increases in ROS production. However, digesta of FFM with CuCD at 0.1% and 1% wt/wt significantly increased ROS production (1.17-fold, p < 0.01; and 1.48-fold, p < 0.001, respectively). It is known that Cu induces ROS through Haber-Weiss and Fenton reactions by generating superoxide and hydroxyl radicals (Zeeshan et al., 2016). Persistent ROS generation has been mechanistically linked to chronic inflammation and tissue injury and is one of the most studied mechanisms of ENMs toxicity (Khatri et al., 2017; Rubio et al., 2019). For example, our previous report has shown that the two most commonly used nano-enabled food additives, TiO2 (E171) and SiO2 (E551), at FDA allowable concentrations in FFM (1% w/w) induced 1.20- and 1.11-fold increases in ROS, respectively (Cao et al., 2019c). It is worth noting that the ROS level induced by CuCD at 0.1% wt/wt is similar to that induced by TiO2 (E171), however, the ROS level induced by CuCD at 1% wt/wt is considerably higher than either nano-enabled food additive. These results support the current understanding in the available literature that pristine CDs are relatively non-toxic and that the cytotoxicity of CDs was dependent on the selection of doping molecules or the surface functionalization (Wang et al., 2011; Havrdova et al., 2016).

It is worth noting that the cytotoxicity of ions of Fe and Cu in human small intestinal epithelial cells are well documented in the literature. It has been reported that ions of Fe showed cytotoxicity (Caco-2 cell viability and proliferation) at a concentration above 1.5 mmol/L (He et al., 2008). This concentration is 10 and 100 times higher than the concentrations of dissolved Fe in this study of FeCD treatments at either concentration of 0.1% or 1% wt/wt, respectively. This explains the lack of cytotoxicity found in FeCD exposures at tested concentrations in this study. For copper, cytotoxicity (LDH release and cell proliferation) was found in the literature at a concentration above 0.1 mmol/L (Zödl et al., 2003). This concentration is 1.5 times higher than the concentration of ionic Cu levels found in of the case of CuCD at starting food concentration of 0.1% wt/wt. For the starting concentration at 1% wt/wt, the ionic Cu was found 7 times higher than 0.1 mmol/L. Therefore, the cytotoxicity observed in this study can be attributed to the dissolved Cu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular metabolome impact of ingested CDs on triculture epithelial cells
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the impact of ingested CDs on cellular metabolome, we performed untargeted metabolomic analysis by gas chromatography mass-spectrometry (GC–MS) for FFM, CD and CuCD groups. A total of 70 unique metabolites were identified and an additional 121 peaks were detected but not identified in the samples. Statistical comparisons between the CD and FFM groups revealed that just one unknown metabolite showed a statistically significant (ANOVA, p-value<0.05) change in abundance, indicating minimal effect on the cellular metabolome. This result is in accordance with the toxicological results that ingested CD was relatively non-toxic under the tested condition. On the other hand, the CuCD treatment group showed significant changes across 18 metabolites (9 known, 9 unknown) in comparison to the FFM control group. These included metabolites such as d-glucose, L-cysteine, uridine, citric acid and multiple fatty acids as shown in Fig. 6 , indicating oxidative stress and damage in the cells that were exposed to the ingested CuCD. These findings agree with our toxicological results that ingested CuCD induced cytotoxicity, decreased cell viability and increased ROS production.

Pathway mapping of the 9 known metabolites (shown in Fig. 6) to the KEGG Homo sapiens metabolic pathways revealed that the metabolites were involved in pathways including biosynthesis of unsaturated fatty acids, glyoxylate and dicarboxylate metabolism, glycine, serine and threonine metabolism, linoleic acid metabolism, thiamine metabolism, taurine and hypotaurine metabolism, glycerolipid metabolism, pantothenate and CoA biosynthesis, citrate cycle (TCA cycle), pentose phosphate pathway, glycolysis / gluconeogenesis, alanine, aspartate and glutamate metabolism, glutathione metabolism, cysteine and methionine metabolism, fatty acid elongation, fatty acid degradation, pyrimidine metabolism, fatty acid, and aminoacyl-tRNA biosynthesis.

It is known that Cu plays important roles in physiological processes, especially as a cofactor for a number of key metabolic enzymes (Zeeshan et al., 2016). The CuCD treatment group decreased levels of cysteine and uridine, both which are known to have anti-oxidative properties (Cicko et al., 2015; Med Sci et al., 2020; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2019). A decrease was observed in citric acid, a metabolite in the TCA cycle and important intermediate in oxidative metabolism that decreases lipid peroxidation and downregulates inflammation (Abdel-Salam et al., 2014). Glucose metabolism also was impaired, and this is known to occur during oxidative stress (Butterfield and Halliwell, 2019). Fatty acid abundances decreased in the cells that were exposed to the CuCD, indicating altered lipid metabolism and possibly lipid peroxidation. It has also been reported that ROS induced by Cu exposure can stimulate lipid peroxidation (Barata et al., 2005). Therefore, it seems highly likely that the noted effects on the metabolome were presumably induced by the dissolved Cu in CuCD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular proteome impact of ingested CDs on triculture epithelial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Sample processing with mPLEX allows the simultaneous quantification of the metabolome and the proteome. Profiling of the proteomes of the triculture epithelial cells resulted in the identification of 5303 protein groups in total, 3171 of which were quantified in all samples (Supplemental Table S2). The medians of coefficient of variation among biological replicates (n = 4) were less than 10% for the three sample groups, indicating good quantification reproducibility (Fig. S9A). However, no apparent separation by sample type was observed in PCA analysis (Fig. S9B). Indeed, a scaled heatmap of all quantified proteins showed little difference among the three sample types (Fig. 7A). Consistent with these findings, comparative analysis showed no significant change by either the CD (Fig. 7B) or the CuCD (Fig. 7C) treatment. In both cases, the fold change for most proteins was within 20% (indicated by two vertical lines on the volcano plots). Further inspection of the p values of the comparison groups (CD vs FFM, and CuCD vs FFM) showed an almost even distribution, confirming that there neither CD or CuCD treatment affect the triculture proteome (Fig. S9C and D).

The little to no impact on the cellular proteome was in contrast to the statistically significant changes on ROS production, cellular metabolites and cell viability by CuCD. However, such a discrepancy between protein and metabolite abundances and physiological outcomes is not unprecedented. Coverage and sensitivity can vary across different omics measurements and it is possible that small changes in protein abundance or proteins of low abundance were not detected in our untargeted proteomic measurements. Additionally, protein activity could be altered via posttranslational modifications (e.g., phosphorylation, redox modifications) without measurable changes in the abundance (Mann and Jensen, 2003). Further investigation on the mechanisms of metabolite changes is warranted to explain the discrepancy. In our previous study assessing the biological responses of lung macrophages to ENMs, we found that the extent of proteome change is positively correlated with the level of ROS that were generated in response to ENMs (Zhang et al., 2020a). Notably, ENMs that generate statistically significant but low level of ROS (e.g., Fe2O3) led to a minimal impact on the cellular proteome. Thus, it is possible that the level of ROS generated by CuCD is not high enough to trigger a significant or detectable perturbation on the proteome. Given the fact that proteins are susceptible to multiple post-translational modifications, including redox-dependent modifications on protein thiols (Zhang et al., 2020b), it is also likely that such redox modifications are key events that mediate cellular responses to CuCD without any notable changes on the protein expression level.

In summary, this study investigated the physicochemical transformation of CD, FeCD, and CuCD during gastrointestinal digestion, and their cytotoxicity and impacts on cellular metabolome. The results demonstrated that CDs agglomerate in the small intestinal phase, and Fe and Cu are dissolved during simulated digestion. Moreover, CD and FeCD are relatively non-cytotoxic in the triculture human intestinal epithelium under the tested experimental conditions. However, CuCD showed dose-dependent toxic effects on the tested parameters, i.e., TEER, cell viability, cytotoxicity (LDH release), and ROS production. It is worth noting that CuCD also significantly altered multiple important metabolites, such as d-glucose, L-cysteine, uridine, citric acid and multiple fatty acids, indicating oxidative stress and damage in the cells. This information provides a valuable scientific basis for investigations of CDs' potential applications in agri-food industry and its potential implications for human health.
